nusa-penida.summary: Nusa Penida's power system has evolved beyond the legacy wind-diesel record. PLN now reports a 3.5 MWp solar-diesel-battery hybrid commissioned for the 2022 G20 period; published PLN and ministry pages give differing 1.84 MWh and 3.3 MWh storage figures, so both are retained with dates and sources.

nushima-island-dc-microgrid.summary: The Nushima project was a 2012–2014 Japanese government-funded DC microgrid demonstration led by Kobe University. A 360 V DC bus integrated PV, wind, stationary and mobile batteries, direct-DC appliances, converter-fed AC loads, ship-to-grid capability, and demand-response experiments.
nushima-island-dc-microgrid.status: Research demonstration completed after the 2012–2014 funded period

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: 8 kW in the published demonstration description

specifications.label: Battery storage
specifications.value: 46 kWh in the published demonstration description

specifications.label: Reported outcome
specifications.value: Approximately 30% reduction in electricity consumption using DC conversion and demand-response measures

equipment.detail: Controls battery charge/discharge from total power balance and state of charge across a 360 VDC bus; no commercial controller model is identified.
High-frequency-link DC/DC converters, sinusoidal PWM DC/AC inverter, AC/DC grid interface, and converters for stationary/mobile batteries and ship charging.

sources.title: Nushima stand-alone distributed energy demonstration using DC technology
A Verification of High-efficiency DC Micro-grid Power Systems

oneida-nation-of-the-thames.summary: Oneida Nation of the Thames received matching federal and Ontario funding for a community-housing solar microgrid and later completed a 1 MW community solar and 38 kWh storage project with JAZZ Solar Solutions. Current federal reporting frames the next phase as community DER and storage capacity building.
oneida-nation-of-the-thames.status: Community solar and storage completed; ongoing DER and grid-modernization capacity building

pearl-harbour.summary: The Joint Base Pearl Harbor-Hickam SPIDERS Phase I microgrid was a federal cyber-secure controls demonstration integrating diesel generation, solar, uninterruptible loads, black-start capability, and grid-tied export. Federal sources report 150 kW solar and 2.4 MW diesel; the older directory's wind and storage figures are retained separately as unverified historical claims.
pearl-harbour.status: SPIDERS Phase I demonstration completed and operated in the cited period

UPS supported designated uninterruptible loads; manufacturer and energy capacity not stated.

pulau-ubin-microgrid.summary: Pulau Ubin's biodiesel-solar microgrid was commissioned as an Energy Market Authority test bed and built by a two-company Singapore consortium. EMA states that the consortium designed, built, owned, and operated the system for participating residents and businesses.
pulau-ubin-microgrid.status: Supply launched in 2013; continued operation reported in 2014, with present status not independently confirmed

rio-cauca.summary: The recovered directory contains only the name Rio Cauca, approximate coordinates, and aggregate hydro, solar, and diesel ratings. A broad search did not locate a primary project record that uniquely matches those values, so no owner, contractor, equipment vendor, or controller has been attributed.
rio-cauca.status: Legacy directory record; project identity and operating status not independently verified

samso-island.summary: Samsø's renewable-energy-island program is a community transition rather than a single controller-centered microgrid. Local institutions coordinated onshore and offshore wind, district heating, solar thermal, and community ownership; public sources describe the asset fleet but do not name one central microgrid controller.
samso-island.status: Operational island energy system and continuing transition program

sumba-smart-microgrid.summary: Sumba's 500 kWp smart microgrid demonstration was a BPPT–Kyudenko technology-cooperation project. Public reports identify the partners and Smart Energy Management System, but do not disclose the PV-module, inverter, battery, or controller model numbers.
sumba-smart-microgrid.status: Demonstration commissioned in 2017; present operating status not independently confirmed

thabazimbi-chromium-mine.summary: The Thabazimbi chrome-mine hybrid plant combines a 1 MW PV array with off-grid diesel generation. SMA identifies its Sunny Tripower inverters and Fuel Save Controller; project reporting identifies Cronimet as mine operator/developer and Solea Renewables as turnkey EPC.
thabazimbi-chromium-mine.status: Operational from November 2012 in the cited project period; current mine status not independently confirmed

Hybrid power plant reduces mine operational costs

alcatraz-island-microgrid.summary: Alcatraz Island's National Park Service microgrid combines a 305 kW photovoltaic array, lead-acid storage, diesel generation, bidirectional power conversion, and supervisory controls. Current federal sources document the operating concept and outcomes; the archived Princeton Power case study supplies controller and inverter detail.
alcatraz-island-microgrid.status: Operational National Park Service island microgrid; completed in 2012

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy Federal Energy Management Program

equipment.detail: Coordinates solar, battery, and diesel resources and starts diesel generation when renewable energy and stored energy cannot meet load.
The archived manufacturer case study identifies eight 100 kW-class power-conversion units; the federal page does not restate the model count.
Lead-acid battery bank. The reviewed sources do not disclose a battery manufacturer or cell model.

technicalDetails.detail: The controller prioritizes photovoltaic production, then battery energy, and calls diesel generation when those resources cannot support island load.
DOE reports a verified 305 kW array, while the recovered directory listed 400 kW; both values are preserved with provenance instead of silently selecting one.
Public federal sources do not identify the PV-module, battery-cell, diesel-genset, switchgear, or communications-network model numbers.

sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Energy Federal Energy Management Program

sources.title: Sustainable Alcatraz

de-grussa-copper-mine-microgrid.summary: DeGrussa was a utility-scale off-grid mine hybrid: 10.6 MW AC of single-axis-tracking PV, a 6 MW battery, and an existing 19 MW diesel station serving an 11–13 MW continuous mine load. ARENA's final knowledge report documents the integration controls, operating reserves, participants, and a field communications failure down to the named controller bridge.
de-grussa-copper-mine-microgrid.status: Commissioned in June 2016 for the DeGrussa mine; post-mine operating status is not established by the cited project reports

organizations.name: DeGrussa Solar Project Pty Ltd
organizations.role: ARENA project lead and project company

organizations.name: Sandfire Resources NL
organizations.role: Mine owner and solar power purchaser

organizations.name: Neoen
organizations.role: Solar-battery project owner in the cited operating period

organizations.role: Hybrid-system developer, EPC integrator, and knowledge-report author

organizations.role: Owner and operator of the existing 19 MW diesel power station

organizations.name: ABB
organizations.role: Battery control-system supplier identified in the commissioning report

equipment.detail: Interfaces plant controls; the knowledge report traces intermittent commissioning communications failures to outdated bridge firmware.
Named in the knowledge report's controller-communications incident; the exact ABB product model is not disclosed.
Single 5 MVA battery transformer; manufacturer and model are not stated in the reviewed report.
Coordinates PV, battery, and diesel operating constraints; the cited reports do not name a commercial software release.

technicalDetails.detail: The operating philosophy maintained spinning reserve equal to 1 MW plus 85% of instantaneous PV output across battery and diesel resources.
The battery provides ramp-rate control, frequency support, reactive-power control, voltage support, and spinning reserve for the isolated mine grid.
A controller communications fault was resolved by updating ComAp InteliSys Bridge firmware, a rare publicly documented field-level integration detail.

fort-carson-microgrid.summary: Fort Carson hosted Phase 2 of the U.S. SPIDERS cybersecure microgrid program. The seven-building demonstration combined existing diesel assets, part of a 2 MW PV field, five vehicle-to-grid electric vehicles, motor-operated switching, and IPERC's distributed control architecture; DOE's transition report documents both equipment and black-start sequence.
fort-carson-microgrid.status: SPIDERS Phase 2 demonstration completed in 2013; current field configuration is not established by the cited transition report

organizations.name: IPERC
organizations.role: Distributed microgrid-control designer and master-controller supplier

organizations.name: Smith Electric Vehicles
organizations.role: Supplier of three vehicle-to-grid electric vehicles

organizations.name: Boulder Electric Vehicle
organizations.role: Supplier of two vehicle-to-grid electric vehicles

specifications.label: Existing diesel capacity
specifications.value: Approximately 3.25 MVA across three directly connected assets

specifications.label: Solar connected to microgrid
specifications.value: 1 MW from an existing 2 MW field; 500 kW was available during the cited demonstration

specifications.label: Vehicle-to-grid fleet
specifications.value: Five EVs and five bidirectional chargers

equipment.detail: Distributed intelligent power controllers, master control, HMI/GUI, and supervisory cybersecurity functions; the Fort Carson report does not give a product model.
Five high-capacity bidirectional chargers used the EV fleet as controllable generation and storage resources.

equipment.manufacturer: Smith Electric Vehicles / Boulder Electric Vehicle
equipment.detail: Three Smith and two Boulder vehicles participated in the demonstration; individual battery-pack models are not reported.
Motor-operated sectionalizing switches, generator bypass breakers, automatic synchronizers, and a neutral-deriving transformer enabled island formation; manufacturers are not consistently disclosed.

technicalDetails.detail: The microgrid formed in roughly ten minutes; PV inverters required an additional five to ten minutes before reconnecting to the islanded bus.
IPERC's distributed controllers coordinated local assets under a supervisory master controller instead of relying on one central field I/O point.
The design connected 1 MW of the 2 MW PV field, but the final demonstration had only 500 kW available; the page keeps design and test conditions separate.

sources.title: SPIDERS Phase 2 Fort Carson Technology Transition Consolidated Report
SPIDERS: Smart Power Infrastructure Demonstration for Energy Reliability and Security

hartley-bay-microgrid.summary: Hartley Bay was Canada's first smart remote microgrid demonstration, adding demand-response controls and feeder instrumentation to the Gitga'at Nation's diesel system. Government technical papers document the generator sizes, 600 V-to-25 kV distribution architecture, controllable building loads, and project collaborators in substantially more detail than the recovered directory.
hartley-bay-microgrid.status: Smart remote microgrid demonstration commissioned; cited 2012–2013 reports do not establish the current control configuration

organizations.name: Gitga'at First Nation
organizations.role: Community host and local system stakeholder

organizations.name: Natural Resources Canada CanmetENERGY
organizations.role: Project lead, research, and technical integration

organizations.name: Aboriginal Affairs and Northern Development Canada

specifications.label: Diesel generation
specifications.value: Two 420 kW gensets plus one 210 kW genset (1.05 MW total)

specifications.label: Community distribution
specifications.value: Approximately 2 km at 25 kV, stepped down to 120/240/208 V

specifications.label: Community scale
specifications.value: Approximately 170 residents and 82 buildings in the project report

equipment.detail: Networked controllers and adjustable 30 A thermostats were deployed at the school, health clinic, and community centre; commercial model numbers are not disclosed.
Monitoring and load-management system added or removed controllable loads according to a system-efficiency algorithm.
600 V generation, step-up to 25 kV community distribution, and building-level step-down service. Transformer and genset manufacturers are not named.

technicalDetails.detail: The controller shifted thermostatically controlled building loads according to generator efficiency and available system margin rather than using battery storage.
The recovered project page reported a 61.3 kW peak reduction and about 77,000 litres of annual diesel savings; these remain archive-sourced figures.
The government paper documents 1.05 MW of installed diesel generation; the recovered directory's 3 MW figure is retained separately and should not be treated as the same verified configuration.
The reviewed smart-microgrid papers do not document an operating 900 kW hydro plant, so the recovered hydro value is not presented as installed equipment.

The First Canadian Smart Remote Microgrid: Hartley Bay, BC

ramea-newfoundland-microgrid.summary: Ramea's isolated power system evolved from a wind-diesel installation into a 2007–2011 wind-hydrogen-diesel research initiative. A peer-reviewed open-access engineering paper supplies rare component-level detail—including Caterpillar diesel ratings, Windmatic turbine generator sizes, capacitor banks, dump load, and governor modes—while the provincial record defines the later hydrogen scope.
ramea-newfoundland-microgrid.status: Historical wind-hydrogen research configuration; current hydrogen-system operation is not claimed

organizations.name: Newfoundland and Labrador Hydro
organizations.role: Project proponent, isolated-system owner, and operating-data provider

organizations.role: Participant named in the recovered project record; precise contractual role was not recovered

organizations.name: Environment Canada
organizations.role: Operating-data contributor acknowledged by the engineering study

specifications.value: Three 925 kW, 4.16 kV, 1,200 rpm generator sets

specifications.value: Six Windmatic WM15S turbines; each carries 65 kW and 13 kW induction generators

Typical load
specifications.value: Approximately 650 kW average; reported winter peak up to 1.1 MW

equipment.detail: Three 925 kW, 4.16 kV units at 1,200 rpm with AVR and governor controls; the paper also identifies the 1,400 hp 3512 engine family.
Six machines, each with 65 kW and 13 kW induction generators.
Coordinates multiple gensets operating with speed-droop and isochronous governor modes; a commercial controller model is not given.

Electrolyser, three storage tanks, and an internal-combustion hydrogen generator were within the provincial research scope; manufacturers and model numbers are not disclosed on the cited project page.

technicalDetails.detail: Automatic voltage regulators and governor controls support droop and isochronous operation, with a diesel master controller coordinating unit commitment.
The original induction-generator wind plant used six switched 30 kvar capacitor banks and a 200 kW controllable dump load for isolated-grid operation.
The paper's 150 kW pumped-hydro system is a modeled proposal, not documented installed equipment, and is deliberately excluded from the equipment list.
Provincial filings describe wind-powered electrolysis, hydrogen storage, and later electricity production through an internal-combustion hydrogen generator.

thabazimbi-chrome-mine-microgrid.summary: The Thabazimbi chrome-mine hybrid power plant couples a 1 MW photovoltaic field to two 800 kVA diesel generators. SMA's official project sheet identifies all 63 inverter units, the Fuel Save Controller, nominal inverter capacity, planner/EPC entities, commissioning date, and expected annual generation.
thabazimbi-chrome-mine-microgrid.status: Commissioned in November 2012; present mine operating status not independently confirmed

Two 800 kVA units; manufacturer and genset model are not disclosed in SMA's project sheet.

tilos-microgrid.summary: Tilos is an EU Horizon 2020 smart-island demonstrator built around an 800 kW wind turbine, 160 kW of PV, and an 800 kW / 2.88 MWh sodium-nickel-chloride battery. The CORDIS record identifies the coordinator and full consortium, while an open-access engineering paper documents battery modules, inverter models, transformers, and grid-connected/islanded operating modes.
tilos-microgrid.status: Horizon 2020 project closed in January 2019; hybrid station described in the 2021 engineering paper

Two 500 kVA bidirectional inverter systems couple the battery containers to the plant.
Two 630 kVA, 20/0.3 kV units; the academic paper does not identify their manufacturer or model.
Optimizes storage for renewable penetration, stability, guaranteed exports, and ancillary services; the reviewed sources do not provide a commercial controller model.

technicalDetails.detail: The hybrid station supports both grid-connected operation with the Kos-Kalymnos system and autonomous island operation.
The research program integrated demand-side management and distributed domestic-hot-water thermal storage with the electrical microgrid.
The battery was designed for energy management, renewable maximization, grid stability, guaranteed export, and ancillary services.
CORDIS lists the coordinator, 16 participating organizations, ended participations, and a third-party entity; the page preserves those distinctions.

intelligent-dc-microgrid-living-lab.summary: Aalborg University's iDClab was a 2014–2017 research program for residential and commercial DC microgrids. Its peer-reviewed living-lab paper describes a side-by-side 380-to-12 V DC apartment and 230 V AC reference apartment, ZigBee home controls, Kamstrup multi-utility metering, renewable generation, batteries, and hydrogen-based micro-CHP.

equipment.detail: Integrates electricity, gas, and district-heating meters so the energy-management system can coordinate whole-building energy use.
Low-bit-rate wireless devices communicate through a gateway to the building energy-management system.
Electrolyser, hydrogen storage, and fuel cell provide electricity and useful heat; commercial models are not disclosed.

equipment.category: Renewable and electrical storage test assets
equipment.detail: PV, small wind turbines, and batteries are included in the laboratory architecture, but the paper does not provide fielded model numbers for each asset.

technicalDetails.detail: Two fully functional residential sections—one multi-bus low-voltage DC and one conventional AC—were designed for direct efficiency comparison.
The EMS architecture spans electricity, gas, and district heating and can choose between battery storage and hydrogen production during renewable surplus.
The program studied design, modeling, control, coordination, communications, and management rather than documenting a commercial production microgrid.
The reviewed paper does not name PV modules, battery cells, DC/DC converters, fuel-cell stack, electrolyser, or central EMS software model.

kwig-alaska-microgrid.summary: Kwigillingok's high-penetration wind-diesel-heat microgrid uses five 95 kW Windmatic turbines, distributed electric-thermal-storage heaters, a load regulator, smart metering, Frontier's supervisory control, and a later battery integration. Alaska Energy Authority and DOE records separate the installed Kwigillingok equipment from broader Chaninik Wind Group results.
kwig-alaska-microgrid.status: Operational project in the cited state and DOE reporting periods; later technical assistance documented maintenance needs

organizations.name: Alaska Energy Authority
organizations.role: State project partner and case-study publisher

organizations.name: Frontier Power Systems
organizations.role: Microgrid control, SCADA, and battery-integration provider

organizations.name: U.S. Department of Energy
organizations.role: Chaninik program funder and later technical-assistance sponsor

specifications.label: Wind generation
specifications.value: Five 95 kW turbines; 475 kW total rated capacity

specifications.label: Electric thermal storage
specifications.value: 27 residential units in the Alaska Energy Authority project sheet; 20 remained in the 2019 technical-assistance account

equipment.detail: Five 95 kW units, 17 m rotor diameter, 24.4 m hub height.
System master, distributed asset controllers, SCADA, and fast load-regulator functions integrate wind, diesel, thermal storage, and battery resources.
Residential heat-storage units absorb surplus wind energy; manufacturer and heater model are not stated in the reviewed state project sheet.
Integrated in 2015 to enable diesel-off operation; the recovered record supplies power and energy values but reviewed public sources do not identify the battery or inverter manufacturer.

technicalDetails.detail: At high wind output, the smart-grid controller diverts surplus electricity to metered residential thermal-storage heaters instead of curtailing all excess generation.
Frontier reports that the battery and control-system expansion allowed the community to shut down diesel generation during suitable wind and load conditions.
A 2019 assistance report found one of five turbines inoperable and arranged peer troubleshooting and Yup'ik-language resident training on thermal-storage use.
DOE's 30% power-plant fuel reduction is a multi-community Chaninik result and is not presented as a Kwigillingok-only measured value.

sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Energy Office of Indian Energy

princeton-university-microgrid.summary: Princeton University's campus microgrid is anchored by a 15 MW General Electric LM1600 combined-heat-and-power plant, a multi-megawatt solar field, chilled-water thermal storage, and real-time economic dispatch. University sources document its island operation during Hurricane Sandy and its current transition toward expanded solar, geo-exchange, heat pumps, and hot/cold thermal storage.
princeton-university-microgrid.status: Operational and being modernized as part of Princeton's net-zero campus program

organizations.name: Princeton University Facilities
organizations.role: Microgrid owner, operator, and campus utility

specifications.label: Gas turbine
specifications.value: General Electric LM1600, natural-gas or diesel capable

specifications.label: Campus electric demand in 2014 account
specifications.value: Approximately 16 MW average; 10 MW night minimum; 27 MW hot-day peak

specifications.label: Hurricane Sandy island output
specifications.value: Approximately 13 MW for about a day and a half

equipment.detail: Dual-fuel aeroderivative gas turbine drives the 15 MW generator; exhaust heat produces campus steam.
Real-time model monitors electricity and fuel prices plus campus electric, steam, and chilled-water demand to guide operators; vendor and software release are not disclosed.
2.6 million gallons of chilled water shift chiller load to lower-cost hours; newer TIGER and CUB facilities add separate hot- and cold-water storage tanks.
Gas-turbine exhaust heats water to make steam for heat, hot water, sterilization, and steam-driven cooling; equipment model is not stated.

technicalDetails.detail: Operators import or export power based on real-time economics and can disconnect the campus to run as an electrical island during utility outages.
After a roughly 20-minute interruption and turbine restart, the campus islanded at about 13 MW until reconnection shortly before midnight on October 31, 2012.
The gas turbine produces electricity while exhaust heat supplies steam; the district-energy plant also dispatches steam- and electric-driven chillers.
The upgraded microgrid is adding solar, geo-exchange, heat pumps, natural-gas backup generation, and paired hot/cold thermal-storage tanks at TIGER and CUB.

sources.title: The Princeton Energy Plant
Two years after Hurricane Sandy, recognition of Princeton's microgrid still surges
Path to Net Zero: New Energy Facilities

tuntutuliak-kongiginak-kwigillingok-micr.summary: The Chaninik Wind Group deployed related but electrically separate wind-diesel-heat smart grids in Kongiganak, Kwigillingok, and Tuntutuliak. DOE's final report and project pages document five Windmatic turbines per completed village, heat-recovery boilers, 21–30 residential thermal-storage installations per village, advanced metering, and a communications chain linking meters, plant supervisory control, and heater controllers.
tuntutuliak-kongiginak-kwigillingok-micr.status: Three-village program completed by 2013; this grouped record does not imply the communities are electrically interconnected

specifications.value: 200,000 gallons of diesel across electricity and heating uses

equipment.detail: Five roughly 95 kW machines in each of the three completed village systems; DOE's early project page rounded the unit rating to 90 kW.

Wind-diesel supervisory control, SCADA, load regulation, and integration services; the grouped sources span multiple installation years, so one controller hardware revision is not assigned to all villages.
Advanced meters distinguish discounted surplus-wind energy from diesel-generated electricity and communicate availability to residential controls.
Networked residential stoves capture surplus wind as heat; unit counts vary by community and report date, and commercial heater models are not disclosed.
Community heating assets also help balance the high-penetration systems; boiler manufacturers and ratings are not stated in the reviewed DOE page.

technicalDetails.detail: The communities are not connected by road or electrical intertie; each village operates its own diesel microgrid under a shared program design.
Advanced meters, the power-plant supervisory controller, and thermal-stove controllers exchange surplus-wind availability and customer-use data.
The smart-meter architecture enabled surplus wind sold as residential heat at approximately half the cost of diesel heating in the DOE project design.
DOE reported about 30% power-plant fuel reduction and at least 30% heating-fuel reduction for participating ETS homes across the operating systems; these are program-level figures.
DOE's planning page states 90 kW per turbine, while later completion reporting identifies 95 kW units; the page labels the time basis rather than blending the ratings.

tokyo-metropolitan-microgrid-high-reliab.summary: The recovered directory preserves only a generic Tokyo title and no facility identifier, organizations, equipment, capacity, or source document. Searches across Japanese public and industry records did not produce a unique match, so this page deliberately avoids merging it with an unrelated Tokyo smart-energy project.
tokyo-metropolitan-microgrid-high-reliab.status: Unresolved legacy record; project identity and operating status not verified

uc-irvine-microgrid.summary: UC Irvine operates a campus microgrid and research test bed served from Southern California Edison's 66 kV network. University documentation identifies the central plant, thermal storage, enterprise energy management, detailed metering, ETAP model, EV charging, fuel cell, and generic-controller research partners.
uc-irvine-microgrid.status: Operational campus microgrid and active research platform

organizations.name: UCI Advanced Power and Energy Program (APEP)
Southern California Edison
organizations.role: Serving utility and generic-controller research partner

organizations.name: MelRoK, LLC
organizations.role: Enterprise energy-management, metering, and demand-response supplier

organizations.name: ETAP
organizations.role: Electrical-network modeling software and controller-research partner

organizations.name: Yazaki
organizations.role: Absorption-chiller supplier for the fuel-cell CCHP system

specifications.label: Electric service
specifications.value: 66 kV stepped to 12 kV by two 15 MVA transformers across ten 12 kV circuits

specifications.label: Combined-cycle plant
specifications.value: 13 MW gas turbine plus 6 MW steam turbine

specifications.label: Solar PV
specifications.value: More than 4 MW in current university description; archived record documented 893 kW

specifications.label: Controller-test battery
specifications.value: 2 MW / 500 kWh deployed for islanding and reconnection research

equipment.detail: Collects meter data, supports Auto-Demand Response, and feeds real-time information to the campus microgrid model.
Calibrated campus electrical model used for steady-state, dynamic, and control studies; not itself the field controller.
APEP, SCE, ETAP, and MelRoK developed and tested a controller against the UCI model and OPAL-RT platform; a commercial model name is not stated.
University page reports 28 public Level 2 chargers in one section; older table values are retained only in source context.
300 kW molten-carbonate fuel cell integrated with a 40-ton Yazaki absorption chiller.

wesleyan-university-microgrid.summary: Wesleyan University's central campus microgrid distributes electricity from the Vine Street substation and integrates campus cogeneration, solar generation, and central thermal systems. The university's current energy page supplies updated ratings that differ from the older directory snapshot.
wesleyan-university-microgrid.status: Operational campus microgrid; long-term renewable conversion planning underway

organizations.name: Wesleyan University
organizations.role: Owner and operator of the campus microgrid and energy assets

organizations.name: Hale Hill Biofuels
organizations.role: B20 biofuel supplier for campus buildings since 2013

specifications.label: Named solar installations
specifications.value: 750 kW Long Lane, 200 kW Freeman Athletic Center, 7.2 kW at 19 Fountain Avenue, and 3 kW at Admissions

specifications.label: Archived aggregate generation
specifications.value: 950 kW solar and 3.8 MW gas/diesel in the earlier directory snapshot

specifications.label: Microgrid service scope
specifications.value: Electricity distributed to approximately 1.8 million square feet of central campus

equipment.detail: The reviewed Wesleyan sources describe the microgrid and generation assets but do not identify a commercial microgrid controller, EMS, inverter, or genset model.
